Shawn — 0:26
we’ll do that. All right. So welcome, everybody to the momentum builder workshop, lean addition, q&a call. We don’t have a time in mind for this. So we’ll just go really, as long as we have questions, knows how long that’s going to be. So a few people sent questions in by email, I’m going in some people who can’t attend for a variety of reasons. So I’m going to read the first question. And this, this has been asked by other people. And I don’t know if it’s been asked in the comments. I know I’ve answered this question already. But I’ll answer it again. And Andre can jump in as well. So here’s the question, How can I adjust the whole momentum builder workshops system to Google ads, I learned a lot about Google ads in the traffic engine course. But it’d be great if you could give further tips for sending traffic to the landing page via Google ads. I would particularly want to know if I can use the same landing page for both Facebook ads and Google ads. Okay, so the answer is like everything else? It depends. It depends on the context. So if you wouldn’t, if we think about Facebook, the the primary distinction between Facebook traffic and Google traffic, is that his intent? So in Google, the intent is active, you’re searching for something in that moment, you want an answer to something on Facebook, you’re there’s passive potential interest. We hope that through a combination of targeting, and other factors in our hook and other other things to mix together, that we capture somebody’s attention, it’s just the right time for them. Those are two wildly different scenarios. I don’t I don’t know that they could be more different. So the initial answer is no, you can’t just build something for Facebook start to finish and then just assume that it’s going to work on Google ads. However, this is where the nuance comes in with it depends. It certainly depends on what your offer is, and you’re in how you’re in how you’re presenting your offer. But what the way to think about Google ads is how specific is the search? Because of the in what is your offer? Is it? Is it time bound? Is there is there a need as you know, something Perry Marshall use the colleges, the prospect of a bleeding neck like they need the solution right now. So if they if the search intent is you suggest that they have this need for something right now, and then you take them through this journey over seven or eight days, you’re just going to piss somebody off, right? It’s just the example I think I use in the traffic engine. I’ve know I’ve used in other places, which is, you know, if somebody has a leaky roof, and there’s the search for roofing contractors near me, you don’t send them to a funnel where they can download the guide to get you know how to save 10% off their next their next Roof Repair. Like that’s not what they want, they want the phone number to call to get the roof fixed. So depending on your offer, and depending on the intent expressed in search, so if it’s a very specific search that shows clear intent that they need a solution right now, we don’t want to take them on a seven plus day journey. However, if the cert if the intent is less specific, so if they’re more you’re just starting to learn about something so we can use an example we’ll use an AutoResponder Madness. If somebody were to search for, you know, something very specific search for AutoResponder Madness, for example, or search for learn email marketing today. I’m making things up. These aren’t great examples. But there would be it really in all of these scenarios, I think we would take them through a very similar funnel. So I chose a bad example, which is always fun for my first my first answer to a question. So let’s let’s go back to the beginning with AutoResponder Madness. Let me see if I can make this a better answer to the question. If somebody searches for learn email marketing, there’s no immediate buying intent. There’s no bleeding neck in that there’s nothing in that search term that suggests they need a solution right now. They’re early in the research phase. So because of that, it would be very appropriate to send to have a certain ad that leads directly to the the landing page that you’ve constructed as part of the momentum builder workshop. But if your product or service is not like that, it’s something that people might want to buy immediately. And you’re bidding on search terms that suggests they want that thing right now, then you don’t want to create a lot of friction in between you would in that. In that case, I would probably send somebody right to your sales page, like if someone is clearly showing buying intent, send them to a sales page and let them buy. If they’re if there’s no clear intent, if it’s research based terms, or if it’s general terms or top of the funnel type, they’re just starting to learn about something, then then it would make more sense. So it depends. It’s not a great answer, but it is, it’s the accurate answer. And there was a follow up on this question, too. And then I’m gonna hand it off to you, Andre. So here’s a question.

So what do I need to be careful about in my Google search ad? Regarding the theme, for example, should I be giving clues about the theme and the search ad? In general? Probably no. Right? So a successful search ad matches the intent of the person searching and makes it clear that you’re the right answer for that for whatever they’re searching for. That’s the job of a search ad. So we’re not introducing a theme at that point, we’re not the only theme that we’re implying is that we’re a useful answer to the search that they’ve just heard the question they just asked, I tend to think about searches. Somebody’s asking a question, how do i Where do I What’s the best? Even if they don’t include that in the search term? It’s often implied. Like when someone types in email marketing, what they probably mean is how do I learn about email marketing? There’s an implied question, or what do I need to know about email marketing? Where do I start with email marketing, they don’t put all of that stuff in the search term, they just start very broadly. So you don’t, when you with Google ads, you don’t need to worry about setting up the theme. That’s the con, the reason that’s so important. And now that that would be present in everything else that follows. But the reason that’s so important with Facebook is in Facebook, nobody’s looking for you. That’s the critical distinction in search. Somebody is looking, they’ve raised their hand, they say, I want to know more about this, right now, in this moment, that intent is wildly different than someone’s scrolling through their Facebook newsfeed think, Oh, that looks interesting. Let me check it out. That’s why we need 800 500 800,000 words in a Facebook ad, to really make it clear what it’s all about and why it’s interesting to the person who’s reading it. That’s not true. With Google as much, because that person has in that moment already said, I’m interested. I’m looking, show me something. Anything that contribute to that laundry? Shawn — 10:02
So Facebook is a CPM environment cost per 1000 impressions in, it can be a cost per click, but very rarely. So Facebook is CPM in general search is cost per click. So, but it’s still, it’s not that cut and dry that we will work. Facebook is different in that you’re there we have there, they’re very likely going to see the image in the first two lines of the ad. And the the job of the image in general, is to stop the scroll. So if you think about the fate, the user experience for Facebook is there scrolling down through and that the the image looks interesting, and just interesting enough that it slows them down. With the reason it just so everyone understands. The reason we chose Facebook, for the momentum builder workshop is because it’s infinitely harder to take someone who’s not looking for what you have to offer at all, and capture their attention and then bring them through this journey than it is when someone says, Hey, I need what you have. Show it to me. And don’t, don’t take this as criticism, just take it as like, we want you to succeed, we want you to get a lot of value out of this out of this workshop, and the workshop is designed to produce value through action. And we don’t want you to get in the way of that action. So that’s the broad answer. Here’s the specific answer. So yes, in the traffic engine, I mentioned that a high quality Facebook ad is share worthy. It’s written in a way where it’s independently valuable. So yes, if you, if you followed our suggestions, in week two of the momentum builder workshop, and you created a Facebook ad that way, by definition, it will be shareworthy, it will be interesting, you will have created something that people within your audience will want to share to others. That makes sense. If nobody shares it, it doesn’t mean you did something wrong, though, it’s part of a larger system. If it’s producing customers at the end at some economically viable rate, then it’s successful, it’s part of a larger system. So don’t, don’t get hung up on the, the reverse of local optima, right? We don’t care about local optima, what we care about is the system optimum. So that’s, that’s a sophisticated way or a complicated way of saying, Don’t worry about all the details of the parts, make sure that those parts are contributing and doing what they need to do for the whole. So a byproduct of that an emergent effect of a high quality Facebook ad, often is it gets shared a lot. If it doesn’t, don’t take that to mean you did something wrong and certainly don’t make that a goal. And I think that’s really the question that’s being asked here. André — 25:05
Yeah. Let me think about this. So it comes in, it’s generating leads, and at some point in your system, there’s a way for you to monetize those, those leads, then. Yeah, then I guess. And yeah, you’re right. I’ve seen many, many writers use lead magnets, you know, writers, they, they tend to just to follow what, what they see the big marketers doing? We not particularly, you know, we’re not real fans of lead magnets, where you were bribing people. You know, it works. So, you know, if you want to use that as your starting point, maybe that’s, that could be, be your be your baseline, and then it’s like, Okay, what does version two look like? How can you replace a lead magnet with something that’s more where somebody can see see the value where you can demonstrate the value where they can experience the value before they give you anything, like the email address. So I’ve thought about this. For for fiction authors, and you know, this, there’s no reason why you can’t have an maybe chapter one have a short story. Or, or like a prequel of other book one. So. So Book One is something that maybe cost money and it’s on Amazon. So you would you would purposefully Write, write a very short, either a short story or, or a novella. And you could put chapter one on the website, they for free, nothing, you know, they can consume it in at the end of chapter one. At that point, you know, there’s an opt in, it’s like, if you want to see chapter two, and the rest of it, it’s a, it’s a, let’s just say it’s a 11 Chapter, book, it’s, it’s free. But here’s chapter one, CPU, CPU diggit. Because now at that point, you know, readers are reading it, some readers are going to be attracted to the character to the, to the hook to the premise of the story to the theme to whatever and they’re going to be, and they can engage in that story in their chapter one. And those people that opt in for the rest of that story, well, they the best leads you could possibly have for your for your paid book. Because they’ve they’ve they’ve demonstrated that way, you don’t get that that dynamic playing out, at least, that signal is less clear. If you just put a freebie on the website and say you need to opt in to get my my lead magnet, which happens which which could still be the heart of the book of the free book, but they’ve not read anything at that point, they may opt in download this thing, read chapter one and think it’s, you know, this isn’t for me, and now you’ve got them on your email list. They somebody that’s not engaged, and they’re never going to be engaged. So, you know, we, personally, that’s we, we tend not to do that. I think that would be my initial riff.

Shawn — 28:42
Because you’re talking I changed my answer, like for glad that you, you tackle this one first. The way I the way I would think about this is what does somebody get when they’re on your email list? I would start there. You know, Stephen Covey said this, and we borrow his idea begin with the end in mind. That’s, that’s really what we’ve done in this workshop is in order to know, we have to know where we’re taking somebody, eventually, to know everything about the journey. So where I would start by thinking about this is okay, when somebody joins your weekly news newsletter, what’s the what? What value does that provide? You know, it’s most likely entertainment. So how like, just dimensionalize that like, what’s like, what kinds of characters is it? You know, thriller, spy novel, romance it, you’ve got to get clear about that. Like, what, in what need does that fill? What desire does that fulfill? How do they what how does that make them feel differently? I would focus on that. And once I understood that, I would go back I would start with a Facebook ad to frame that again, and this is not how I would say it but the way my mind would be Thinking about when I’m framing it is what? What is it somebody needs to believe or want or a need? Where the value that I’m going to offer them in my email newsletter? Is the answer to that question, because I have to frame. And this is really what so much of this workshop is about is we know where we’re taking somebody. So we’re we’re making a world where the place we’re taking them is the logical conclusion of living in that world, right? And then the Facebook ad is the thing that creates the doorway that they’re going to step through into that world. So in order to do you have to sell this offer this this weekly newsletter, that’s the, that’s the end thing, somebody let’s just for the sake of argument, say it’s a spy thriller, that that’s what you’re writing that that’s the fiction that you write. Well, the journey in between needs to be attractive to people who are interested in spy thrillers, and the ad needs to make it clear that the value you have that you’ve brought to the world is most interesting to people who like spy thrillers. So then the question of do you give them a lead magnet? I like those kinds of they sort of answer themselves along the way. And I don’t know that there’s a definitive answer. Andre mentioned, we don’t tend to love lead magnets. I think one of the reasons we I know one of the reasons I don’t like lead magnets is it’s it’s a false. It’s a false sense of accomplishment when somebody downloads you know, they see something, it’s, oh, this is interesting. And they, they get a little squirt of dopamine, when they opt in, and they download the thing that they just, they were promised, and that little squirt of dopamine of it oftentimes is an end unto itself. They don’t even open it. So I like I like to for i like to force consumption of ideas, is really what it gets down to. So for you, I would be more inclined to have the the short story as part of the journey, you can do it as a broken up over the relationship building emails, like a little preface, and then off you go, you could do it on the landing page, you could you could, you could start the story in the Facebook ad, you have some kind of frame, yeah, this is who you are the right, these particular types of store, or they, whatever it is, and you start this, the story continues on the landing page. And then when they want to keep going in the emails, they have to opt in for the rest, and it’s still free, they still get it, that’s where you present the, you know, sign up for the email, and this is what you get. I mean, there’s lots of ways to do this. But I think it all comes down to do you know the value you’re creating? And who you are creating that value for? Can you dimensionalize that? Can you describe it, when you get my stuff, you your life is different this way, even if it’s entertainment doesn’t matter. This is this is who I am, this is who I create stuff for, and this is what they like about it, then go back to the beginning and build a world where clicking where we’re signing up for that email is the logical conclusion for those people where it’s just that that’s, of course, that’s the next thing that they would do. That’s how I would think about this. Alright, ask you another question. Anything you want to wrap that up at all? Andre? Are you good and move on to the next one?

André — 33:24
No. I mean, I think authors, you know, they, they’ve got a license to really entertain readers, you know, depending on the genre is, you know, readers read books, because they want to lose themselves in in this world. They want to be entertained, they want to get drawn in. And that doesn’t have to start on the book. You know, a reader is somebody that’s seeking out this, this thing. So you could, you know, there’s no reason why authors can’t make that experience that journey start as early as an ad, certainly on the website, like all over it. I’ve, I’ve, I’ve not seen very many examples of this again, at you know, authors aren’t marketers. André — 35:46
It’s pretty nuanced. I would say in the beginning, you don’t need, you know, you need to create these assets. That’s, that’s the point of the workshop. So if you’ve created all the assets beginning to end, okay, that’s there’s an option there to, to perhaps, you know, share these with the professional and get them chiseled and made better. But you don’t necessarily have to do that you could put that love and try and get some some data back, you know, see what’s working. Because at the end of the day, when you hire somebody that cost money, and if you can give them something that’s working, that’s gonna cost you a lot less money, and it’ll help them create something even better. But if you just give them a blank slate, and they ask you, so has this thing been tested? And you say, No, that’s not really helpful. Shawn — 37:41
Thanks. Yeah, this is a this is a hard question. I think. So I can tell you from my experience, so this was probably this was probably eight years of the 21 that I ran an agency I’ve I saw somewhere in the neighborhood of a dozen Facebook ads, that that I call unique. I think in the traffic engine, I mentioned my column unicorn ads that they performed. So pout, they outperformed any expectation any any common sense, realistic expectation of anything. And when measured in terms of sales, like just, if you’re thinking about an ad, that was that was the purpose of the ad was to sell something eventually, whether it was over four part video series, or through a landing page didn’t matter. But there were there were about a dozen that rose to this level, where they the performance was if someone had told me the performance, I would have, I would have said it was not believable. I mentioned this because the thing every one of them had in common is they were not written by professionals. They were each written by the person who either own the business or was somehow directly responsible for the business. And my conclusion from that was that they’re particularly in Facebook. Facebook is a social environment, social environment, where we’re most of the content that we consume on Facebook, that’s not advertising is written by another human being who’s not a professional writer. And when we inject into that, you know, quote, unquote, copywriting it sticks out immediately, like an ad it feels like an ad we know and we’re finely tuned as a species to know when we’re when when something is an ad versus not. So this is all a very circuitous way to come back to this point that what to you feels like unpolished writing and maybe not, not great writing is most likely in a an environment like Facebook, incredibly authentic, you probably sound very real and authentic. anticipate it in my observation is is something that can’t be necessarily engineered. I haven’t ever seen anybody engineer authenticity, well, like you have to be authentic. And it’s an emergent property of something else, it’s not really something you can create. And when you try to create, it looks like you’re trying to create it. So that’s my way of saying, give yourself a break. Follow what Andre said, First, put your stuff out in the world at the best that you can create it and see what happens. Because my, my suspicion is, that’s going to outperform anything any professional ever creates for you, because you know, your audience better, you know, your expertise better. But all of that you have so many advantages. Now, the one caveat here is, I do think there’s value in finding an editor, not a writer, editing is so much different than writing. But to find an editor, and that could be somebody in this in this workshop group, you could have a collaborator, you could edit each other’s work in editing doesn’t have to be sophisticated. It could just be somebody saying to you, I don’t I’m not really sure what you meant here. Can you help me understand that better, or, you know, this thing that you said, here, I felt like you already said this earlier, like this, it’s just somebody to reflect back to you, what, like, how they’re feeling what they’re what they’re taking from your writing, which then allows you to make oftentimes allows you to make very dramatic improvements and cleaning it up in the editing process, I would be far less inclined to try to go find somebody to write and I’ve, I’ve worked with so many clients who’ve hired writers, I’ve worked with writers in the past, it is it is, not only is it very difficult to find a good writer, it is exceptionally difficult to find a good writer who can infer your voice, and who can write in a way that makes that that step feels like what you would write that is so difficult. So that’s a long way of saying the way Andre described it, I would, I would absolutely concur with that. Just do do what you can and see what happens. And then do it you can see what happens. Try to find someone to work with you to edit to clean things up, especially structurally, like there are things flowing, like do that. And then if on the other side of that you’re not getting the results that you want.

Then maybe maybe look around to try to find someone to help with the writing. But that’s, that’s hard. I don’t know. I’m not that doesn’t seem that I know that’s not very reassuring, but that that’s an art. That’s my honest answer of how I would think about it.

André — 50:24

So just to generate low cost customer, a $7 customer and 10, Buck customer, whatever. And, as I think we all know, you know, a customer that has purchased a tripwire is not the same customer that’s going to purchase something that, let’s say cost $500 or $1,000. That’s an assumption that that many marketers make, because they play the numbers game. And that’s not the game that we play, we’re in, we’re in a different business, we’re in this, you know, happy customer generating business. And for that to happen, everything upstream needs to change. And it’s not the same thing. The tripwire at the top of the funnel, at the wide end, you know, that’s a completely different strategy that’s going to generate a completely different type of customer or slash lead. And the things that you’ve got to do, it’s just going to be different. And so it’s a choice you make you can you can do stuff similar to what we do to what to what Seth Seth Godin does. So I think that’s my initial question, or answer.

Shawn — 52:44
And I think to answer this question, we also have to put it have some context, everyone who knows me at this point knows I can’t function without sort of orienting us to where we are in a conversation. So first of all, what’s a tripwire? tripwire is just a low cost front end offer, usually, you know, somewhere under like 20 bucks. So a typical funnel that uses a tripwire will have a low cost offer like free plus shipping. So you get the book for free, but you pay 1095 for shipping, then it has a bump offer. So once you’ve put in your info, you’re like yep, I want to do this as a checkbox that moves the the probably brings the cost up to like $29 or so. So maybe you get if you’re buying a book, maybe get the audio version and a PDF mind map with it. So now your your free thing that you attracted people with just pay shipping, get this book for free, your free offer now that’s the 1295 to cover the shipping and handling. And now you’ve bumped it up to $29. And then they buy it and then they get upsell number one, which is hey, if you like this book, you’re going to get you can do this other thing, which is $49. And then often there’s upsell number two, what’s more in the 250 range. And then all of this is set up eventually a core offer that’s the real thing that you’re selling. In the idea, there’s a couple of things to understand about why people do tripwires one, it’s a way to offset the cost of paying for traffic. So you’re selling something because you’re it’s costing you money to get the person to do to buy and you want to offset the cost of that.

That’s that’s one of the primary reasons the secondary reason I think is a it’s a bait and switch idea that and when I say bait and switch it’s if you if you really understand the you know if you’re if you understand how the world works, then you see the flawed logic if you don’t if you just accept it at face value, it’s not as easy to see the flawed logic so here’s the flawed logic. You’ll often hear people who advocate for the in the term tripwire was coined by Ryan Deiss. A digital marketer is just a low cost front end offer. He just gave it a good name, which is brilliant marketing and became associated with it and everyone thinks that Ryan Deiss invented the tripwire he just gave it a name. So the logic behind it is when somebody has purchased when you when you’re making you’re creating a list have customers, that person is 10 times more likely to buy later. So. And within the direct response world, there’s a bifurcation of email lists, you might have an email list of leads. Or you might have an email list of buyers. And if you have an email list of buyers, that’s thought to be far more valuable. So if you’re in the business of promoting other people’s stuff, and and someone you’re chatting in, someone says, Hey, I’d like to mail you, how much would it cost me to mail onto your list? Well, then you say, if it’s a list of leads, you’re going to get a lot less money than if you have a list of buyers. So because of that, incentives drive behavior. So if you the lowest threshold to get a list of buyers is to sell something inexpensive. So but here’s the flawed logic. The, the assumption is the, the list of low cost buyers is 10 times more valuable than a list of just leads. But that’s a list of crappy leads. So it’s 10 times more valuable than basically zero, right? That’s, that’s where the logic is flawed. And it’s also flawed because people who buy $10 Things tend to want to buy $10 things, especially on Facebook, when the algorithm is optimizing on your behalf. When you’re selling a free plus shipping offer, you tend to find lots of people who want to buy low cost things with really outsized value propositions, you don’t necessarily find $10,000 course buyers, because you sold them a 995 book, I’ve worked with a lot of clients who’ve used free plus shipping book funnels, I’ve talked to an unbelievable number of people who’ve done this and uniformly it does not produce the results in people like Brooke Castillo from The Life Coach School has talked about this publicly when she talked about switching over to a sphere of influence inspired front end system and how much better that was for her than a book funnel. Because the book funnel that was selling $10 ebooks got $10 customers, right in her offers 297 a month, there’s a huge jump between a $10 It’s not the same customer. So this idea that somehow there’s a there’s this, this context independent thing we call a customer. And if you can just make one of those, then that that thing, that person who just became a customer, now I was going to buy your $1,000 offer. That’s bullshit. That’s not how the world works. We all know that intuitively. So when do you know that when you know how the world really works? It’s it’s much easier to answer this question. So yes, if you are doing high volume traffic, where you need to monetize that traffic to to pay for it, I think there’s a case to be made that a low cost offer on the front end makes sense. Just be aware that you’re built, you’re still effectively building yourself a list of leads, that is marginally more likely to buy an offer down the road, you just didn’t have to pay for them. And if you compare that to pay more for your leads up front, it does cost more to get them through, but they’re of an infinitely higher quality, then I just don’t see the value of the tripwire it seems like it adds a lot of heat but no light. It just there’s not there’s not a lot of benefit for me. From my perspective and sort of seeing it in action. So doesn’t mean don’t do it. There’s some there’s some context where it just it makes sense. You know, like if you’re doing some kind of I don’t know, there’s some places where it makes sense. I guess, the fact that I’m not thinking of any, but just to also to add something interesting. We’ve never used a tripwire. I don’t know that Andres ever used the tripwire long before I showed up. I can I cannot see any scenario in the future where we will be using a tripwire I can’t imagine that scenario. So that’s a long answer to a short question. Anything you want to throw in there before we jump to the next one?

André — 59:09
Yeah, I think the only thing I’ll add is the type of marketing that we do. And we we do and then as a byproduct of that we also teach is it’s heavily front loaded with world building where we, where we demonstrate value upfront before asking for anything, any transaction. And as Sean said, it’s a different type of person that that makes their way through that and and is attracted and pulled through that and then they raise the hand and they want to opt in. You don’t need to put a tripwire in that scenario. In fact, it’s, you know, again, I’m just making an assumption here, but I think it could it it would not improve the system. It’s, you know, what we’ve what we’ve shown inside of the in this workshop is a funnel. You know, he asked, What else? Can we build a funnel House House? Can we build a funnel? Well, we’ve shown you how to build a funnel that can work all day long, it’ll generate high quality leads. And if you have something to sell those leads, you can sell stuff to the leads. That’s the funnel. And then once that thing’s working, you just expand your world and expand it. But that is a funnel for lack of a better term.

He’s selling the book so that the lead gets more. There’s there, he’s educating them about what’s possible. So I think Robin is a really good example of where, where you can pull this off and pay for some of your traffic, maybe pay for all of your traffic. It’s just what happens after that, it’s recognizing that anytime we introduce something, we’ve changed the context for the prospect or the customer. So when we, when we bring somebody through from a long form Facebook ad to a landing page to opt in for what’s next. That’s a certain context, when we’ve we’ve brought somebody into a world where we’ve positioned a book as being a resource in a particular topic, we and someone says I want to buy that. Now, we’ve changed their world a little bit now that we’ve given them something to engage with. So I would have like after that I would have a like three or four part, you know, series that, that points out things in the book in order that to draw their attention to like, you know, really, because now you know what’s happened, they now own the book, the context has changed. They now also see Robin as an authority. So the context is changed. And now Robin is acting on that authority, and drawing their attention back to the what the value they can get in the book in ways that are very helpful. He’s guiding them through it, that that changes things dramatically. So thank you, Robert, for giving me a perfect example I never would have come up with on my own. André — 1:04:20
I’m not specifically but, you know, I don’t know how helpful that question really is. Because, you know, I don’t think anybody’s building a business like Ryan does. He’s, when I started in 2003, Ryan Deiss was on the scene. And he had started maybe around about the same time, maybe a few years earlier. He’s been he’s been building his business and, you know, learning what he’s learning for decades. And, you know, his book, his business is a result of decades of work. Shawn — 1:05:11
Yeah, it’s a lot easier to build your business and an idea before you tell the entire world about the idea. That’s, that’s always the danger. If you if you figure out something that works, it’s sort of the Dan Kennedy model, you figured out something that works. And then you sell it. To tell everyone This was his model years ago, you figure out something works enough to explain it, then you sell it to everybody else. But then when everybody else knows, the thing that you did, that works, probably doesn’t really work anymore. So that’s, we’re always, always running backwards. Alright, let me ask you. André — 1:05:57
Anonymous? Yeah, I’m a, I’m a big fan. Big fan of images and sketches. You know, we send many emails that don’t have any images in them at all. I think when when you’re interacting with somebody that’s brand new, never seen you cold leads, I think, good. Good images, or really, or hand drawn sketches is a nice way to draw attention and to be different. So it draws attention gets in, get somebody looking seen? I don’t know. But yeah, I’m a fan of them.

Shawn — 1:06:39
I think the only thing I would add is, images on landing pages Absolutely, is you’re controlling that environment, images and emails, just be aware that if you’re a Mac user, by default, images are displayed in mail. If you’re a Windows user, using like Outlook and other tools like that, by default, images are turned off in emails. So the way to, I think the solution to this is to, if you use an image always include a caption that sells the image so that if somebody is if someone’s on Outlook in a Windows environment and looks down, and there’s a little red X, instead of the image, there’s a caption and text underneath it, that that says what it is, so that they’re inclined to to enable images so they can see it. So that’s a good workaround for email. Just don’t never, never assume an email that people see. They’re seeing exactly how you’ve imagined it. Whereas on a landing page, you can you can rest most likely people are seeing it the way you’ve laid it out. So yeah, and images, certainly, if you can pull off get images, they make sense. No, but don’t use like stock. Like, be careful, your images, say a lot. So if you throw in a few if you throw in the classic image of the, you know, for, like there’s always an Asian person, an African American person, a white person, and then fill in the blanks. Five people in front of a whiteboard, like like, if you don’t use that image, like don’t, don’t don’t use any image like that, that just says I didn’t take this seriously enough. But put some thought into the images that you use. All right,

André — 1:08:13
definitely, definitely do not use stock images. That’s

Shawn — 1:08:18
alright, here’s a question from NEC. All right, NEC, what do you got here? Have you ever used a sequence this length for testing demand? That is before you’ve decided to create a product? Or is that over engineering? Aware? I am I may be stepping on the durable business here. The thoughts on that?

André — 1:08:36
Yeah, you certainly could. So again, yeah, this this, this is this is an element of the durable business where you could where you could take, you know, to test out a new funnel or for if you’re going into any market and you don’t know if anything’s working before you invest time to create an offer and you don’t have an audience. Yeah, you could certainly, you could certainly do that. You could certainly set up a, like a fake front end. It’s, you know, there’s there’s different versions of it, but you’re looking for for signals that that people would maybe spend money, you could do that with a with a button that says you know, get my thing for $100 and when they click on it goes to a landing page that says it’s not quite ready yet. Thanks for raising your hand. If you want to opt in, we’ll let you know as soon as it’s ready. But the fact that that person clicked on a button that says balmy $400 Is is a better signal that they would certainly if you’ve made that price clear on the landing page so it’s not something that’s that’s blind and we needed to click through on the button to see how much the prices you know that’s that’s that’s a decent signal or or profit See that? Whatever you’ve got there is working this. There’s some level of demand. Yeah, it’s nuanced. It’s it’s not really what we what we’re doing in this workshop.

Shawn — 1:10:16
The way I think about this question I had when I’ve ran an agency, I had a rule that I well, I, like I say that I follow a rule that I did not work with clients who had unproven offers. I somehow managed to violate that rule just about every opportunity presented itself, because I get interested in like, Oh, I think we can make this work. So I did a lot of this, which is essentially created, there is no offer yet. There’s no, there’s no, there’s really no, there was no product. And we were trying to validate was there interest enough in the offer to create the product. So having done that a lot, the the thing that I found most useful was that if we engineered the sequence to get people to get on a call, at some sort of dependent, the different ways to that it was presented, it could have been a strategy call exploratory call, sometimes we’re very overt, that it was a way of gathering information then. And if they participated, they would get some benefit in the future. But I found much more value on behalf of my clients, if they were getting, they were having real synchronous conversations with other people. And the signals that we would look for where, you know, certainly leads in like, how many what was the interest initially, booked calls? That was a big one, but the really big one was show up, right? Like, what percentage of people who booked a call actually showed up, that told us a lot about the likelihood that they would buy in the future. And then then it was just getting them on the call. And really having often the business owner or some proxy, who really understood it, never a third party, like research or data gathering, they just didn’t get it. Because you have to really, you have to, you have to be paying attention to the conversations, and in be good at asking follow up questions and really get to have the ability to sense is this person does this person seem like a prospect? Who would would buy does this person see late or mildly interested? Do they? Are they a good fit at all? So I think that I like that model, for using a sequence the way we’ve described in this workshop, that becomes the end goal, that becomes the you, when you make the bridge, email, the bridge, email is, is explaining the value they’re going to get from this conversation strategy, call, whatever it is, and the promotional emails are getting them to get on the phone. And then you’re just assessing whether or not this this is you’re just assessing the people who make it all the way to there. Is there some likelihood that they’re going to buy? I mean, you can I’ve had clients ask to say we’re, we’re working on something. So whatever it’s going to be when we release, it’s going to be 1995. However, because you took the time to help in its creation, I will send you a 50% off discount code. If you’re interested in that, just let me know. And if people send us, you know, thanks or whatever, then that’s that was a very powerful signal compared to people who said, Oh, yeah, absolutely. Please send me that. Like then. It’s anecdotal, you know, and I didn’t I know the phrase, the plural of anecdote is not data. But it’s still very useful. There’s just going to directionally right. Shawn — 1:13:39

Of, I feel that most of the client, I feel like most actually, this is more for me. Alright. I’ll ask the question. I’ll answer it in this way after you. I feel like most of the clients don’t get to play the long game and optimize for happy customers game. They’re looking for short term results, Shawn, with your vast experience of doing client work? How did you work through this? I quit to an agency work? Fine. That’s the honest answer. Yeah, that’s great. This is the All joking aside, this is the one of a few critical problems of being a service provider. There are three and this is one of the big three, which is that clients, even if if they nod up and down and say I get it, I understand why we’re playing the long game. I absolutely get they their actions overwhelmingly don’t follow that they have they are looking for short term results. And this can manifest in lots of ways where they get questions about you know, what was our cost per lead last week and is it going up or is it going down and just the questions that are there just mind numbing. So there you have a few options one, find better customers, right? That’s the hard. That’s hard. And I’m not saying that in a way that’s good at all. I mean that you

do have to be picky. And in interview your questions, it’s a good reframe. I did this for a long time. It’s a wonderful reframe where, so here’s a conventional frame for a service provider, you’re pursuing the client, you want to work for the client, I would reframe that, where the client, it’s, it’s there. And I didn’t ever say it this way with the client is lucky to work with me. And the reality is, if you’re a service provider, you’re being hired, you know, by by definition, you know, more than the client does. So yes, you are the one who should be pursued. If you’re treated like a commodity by the client, it’s a bad client, period. There’s no way around it, there’s no fix for that. So I would recommend, this is a this is a long way to answer this question. But I think this is the most value I can give you product to answer this question as to by David Baker’s book, the business of expertise. He the book in and of itself is masterful. If you run any kind of creative agency or service provider, it was sort of in this marketing world. That book is is the Bible of the Quran, the the fill in the blanks, the Torah, fill in the blanks. It’s It’s so good. And I there’s I cannot even approach explaining the answer this question as well, as David Baker has explained it. So start with that book and recognize that, and he has this model of people have, they don’t provide services unless they’ve done the the strategy consulting work first, and the client is agreeing to implement your strategy. Even on the micro level as an individual service provider, you can pull that off, often you have to educate your clients. And you have to be willing to draw a line in the sand and not violate your own rules, I’ve violated mine more times than I can count. So I’m not saying this from, you know, a holier than thou perspective, it’s just once you make the decision that you are the expert than you need to embody expertise, which is you’re not putting up with bullshit with people who don’t get it, you’re going to give them an opportunity to understand how to win at this game that they’re trying to play. You’re explained there, you’re making it clear that you’re the expert in that game. And if they want to win out, they need to follow your strategy. And if they want to optimize for the short term, they need to go find somebody else who’s going to do that, because you are not willing to you can, the way I would explain it to clients is that I take fiduciary responsibility for their for the relationship, meaning I am I look at their, what they’re spending on adspend. In particular, as I am a steward of that money, and I cannot spend that money in a way that is counter to what they told they’ve told me, their best interests are. And once I framed it that way, it made the conversations much easier. So when a client would say but you know, we want you to do XYZ, because the cost per lead is too high, I would remind them you have not hired me to optimize for cost per lead, you have hired me to get you happy customers. And because of that, that’s how I made making my decisions period and say, well, we want to do X, that point it’s very clear we can we I’m not willing to do it the way that you’ve described, because that will not achieve the goal that you have described to me period. It’s hard to do this. But when clients get it, they eventually leave you alone. Because they know it’s just not worth having a conversation because that they start to buy in. But this is hard. This is incredibly, incredibly hard. Anything you want to throw in that question, Andre want to just move on to the next one.

So I, and I’m not, I don’t want to be very clear. I’m not saying this to suggest in any way, shape or form, that I’m the model of intent of professional integrity, or it’s not, that’s not what I’m saying, at all. I think for me, there’s this phrase that I’ve probably repeated a million times on various calls. And for those of you who’ve heard me say it before, I apologize. But there’s a phrase I get from Graham Duncan in an interview with, with Tim Ferriss where he talks, he said that our our genius is right next to our dysfunction. So I say when I say genius for myself with humility, but part of my genius is I can kind of see the structure of things and how things flow from A to Z. Like if someone explained something to me, and I have enough time to learn to understand it. Or if I read a piece of writing or created, like I see, I can see how it flows. And if there’s something that interrupts that flow, it, that’s where my dysfunction shows up, is that I get I get hung up on it. I can’t. I can’t, I can’t, I can’t make sense of it. It doesn’t flow for me. It doesn’t make sense. So I have worked with clients where I felt like what they were doing and saying didn’t flow. Like they were like something that was being said earlier, maybe promises that were being made. I didn’t see how they resolved later. And because of that, that incongruence It bothers me and it bothers me in a way right? It can be very paralyzing. And I say this not to represent integrity but to represent that this can be very dysfunctional, that I can’t I have a lot there’s I have a lot of difficulty moving forward and making progress if if I sense that the the structure of what I’m doing meaning the The how things are happening doesn’t really make sense. So the best example I can share that sort of an easy example is, I’ve been a martial artist since I was 12 years old, I got my black belt in Korea when I was 16. I continue to train and if I’m in an environment where I’m training, and it feels like the instructor is just kind of doing a flavor of the day, like, let’s throw a bunch of things out and have people go through a bunch of different things with no thought to it, I really struggle in that kind of environment. Because I want to know, like, I, I tend to think to myself that same things I’ve shared in some of my writing, like, first of all, what are we optimizing for here? What’s the point? What are we trying to accomplish? And is what we’re doing moving us in that direction? And if those things don’t line up for me, and if What if what I’m doing doesn’t contribute to what I’m for the reason why I’m doing it. It’s very paralyzing for me in a very weird way. So to bring this back to Cohen’s question, which I hope I pronounced your name correctly. To bring it back to that question, then I would have, I would, it would be very difficult for me to work with a client where I felt like what we were saying anywhere in the journey didn’t match up with a reality. So that’s a very long explanation, very personal explanations, a very long explanation where I don’t think so. But I also, I am absolutely human. I know behavior follows incentives. And I, I wouldn’t be surprised if there haven’t been times where an incentive changed my behavior in ways that if I looked back on it now, I would say, and I wish I could kick that guy in the ass and smarten him up a little bit. Shawn — 1:34:12
All right, next question. I think we’ll just go in order at this point. Is the Facebook ad useful on other social media channels? Maybe. It depends on the channel depends on the length, it’s it’s really the thinking behind the Facebook ad is what’s most important. It’s the ad is setting the frame. And the ads of the ad is doing a few things. It’s it’s pre qualifying. And it’s getting attention for the right people. It’s filtering out the wrong people by how you’ve written it. You’re setting a frame, that frame is the way you will be that like everything else that follows is viewed through that frame. So yes, you might be able to do that on social media channel, other social media channels. Probably the closest one would be LinkedIn although the tone would be a little bit different. The question I would ask, though, is why, if you between Google and Facebook, you’ve got somewhere in high 80 80% coverage of, of all internet users. So in Facebook has. Facebook is the enormous social media audience. So it just, I would, I would do more on Facebook, more ads, more angles, more hooks more like I would do so much more there. Before I would take what I’ve done on Facebook someplace else, a year or two from now, if you have this highly optimized revenue generating machine that you’ve created on Facebook, you know, the hooks that convert the bat, he just all you’ve got the data, you know, it works in, there’s no more there there. You just can’t get more audience on Facebook. At that point, I might go look for other social media channels, but I would not the opportunity cost of looking elsewhere is far too high. relative to what you’re able to do on Facebook, until you’ve really run out of room. That’s short answer. Anything you want to throw in there. Andre, I know you love talking about Facebook advertising.

André — 1:36:04
Yeah, I think you know, between Facebook and Google their control, or the inventory, you really don’t need to go anywhere else. There’s always going to be new social media platforms that that show up. And, and you know, the whole shiny object thing people are always going to rush over. So why why why, you know, follow the crowd and suddenly join all this competition, trying to figure out this new thing to try and monetize it. Where’s you can just stay put? Shawn — 1:36:37
We’ve just explained why I’m not on clubhouse. Alright, next question. Can you provide any guidance on images from a Facebook ad? And should the same image be on the landing page that they clicked to? That’s good question. So I mentioned earlier, the job of the ad is to stop the scroll. In general, that’s part of the job I said, it’s the first job, that’s the first thing it really needs to do is that is to if someone scrolls past your ad, and there’s no chance, so the ad captures the attention, however, like that, that’s what you every Facebook training in the world that’s mentioned. However, the next thing is very important to the Facebook, the image of the ad needs to be somehow related to its evocative of what follows. So if you’re Tai Lopez, and in his crowd, and you’re the you know, you’re standing next to your 15 Lamborghinis in your driveway or whatever they that’s that appeals to his audience, right? The 20 Something primarily young men who think that they’re moments away from internet riches and, and when they make their first half million dollars are going to spend it on a on a Lamborghini. Why would you do that? But whatever? Sure, like that makes, okay, that makes sense. But where it goes off the rails is where it’s like, well, if the job of the image is to stop the scroll, then then the image just needs to be could be funny or cute. No image still is part of the experience. So yes, it has to stop the scroll. No, it shouldn’t be. It shouldn’t look like stock imagery, it shouldn’t look like it shouldn’t look like stock stock imagery, and it shouldn’t look like it’s polished. So it looks like an ad. Those are things you shouldn’t do. If you can avoid them, if it’s a stock image, like arranged in a way that it’s it’s not doesn’t look like you’ve got the whole stock image there, like find part of it or find something. But it needs to be visually interesting and somehow relate. Somehow the person who sees it is trying to figure out how it relates to what they’re reading, if it doesn’t make sense, then that incongruence can be problematic, that lack of congruence can be problematic. So the, this is subtle stuff, but you kind of know it when you see it. And it can be it can be tangentially related. Right? So if I if I were creating an ad like here’s, here’s an ad I actually I’ve run in the past, which was the the ad was talking about building, sort of the mental structure of like how you learn something and then building like this the scaffolding which is a phrase that Charlie Munger, Warren Buffett’s business partner talks about you build a mental scaffolding? Well, the picture in the ad was a building being built. It was a smaller slice of a large image of a building being built and scaffolding wrapping around a corner. Right. So does that. Does that have anything to like? Is that anything to do with the ad? Like literally no, but it was a very subtle reference on the landing page, two, Charlie Munger and this idea of building a mental scaffolding, so it related and it was, it just worked. So you just have to figure that out. As far as do you have to put the same message on the landing page they click through to, I would test it. I don’t know, sometimes it helps. Sometimes it doesn’t. It’s kind of one of those things that’s taken on a life of its own and Facebook advertising circles, where it’s like, oh, you know, it’s gonna, it’s gonna boost. I don’t know, I don’t even know what it’s supposed to boost anymore. If you think about if you have a 1500 word landing page, by the time they they they’re going to take action to the next step to opt in. That image is probably at the top of the page and the ad is probably outside their conscious awareness. At that point, does it make a difference? Maybe? I would try both. I would, I would, I probably would run traffic without it. I would then introduce it for a week or so. And then I would remove it. And I would just look at that. One of the challenges with testing on Facebook is it’s this idea, you never step in the same stream twice. So it’s harder with Google, when you have a pool of people searching for the same search term, different people that same search term, you can kind of from week to week, it’s kind of this it’s kind of the same context. So you can compare week to week Facebook, it’s dynamic social environments, very difficult to say, one week this worked. The next week, it didn’t the following week. It did therefore it’s causal. Hmm. That’s, that’s hard to believe. So not that. It’s a very nuanced answer, but I hope that helps. All right, Andre, next one. My name isn’t a bunch of these owners skip those for now.

Okay, this is from Tim, you’ll like this one. Andre, I think you guys have told this before? What does your daily writing ritual look like? What works well for you and what doesn’t meet their nice

André — 1:41:58
daily writing ritual, it’s starts, the very first thing I started writing. So that’s the first thing for me. If my data isn’t started with writing, then it’s unlikely to get done. My window opportunity or the first few hours of the day, and if I miss that, in fact, I engineered in such a way that I don’t miss that. So I guess that’s, that’s my answer. There’s, there’s an there’s an element of longhand. So you know, journaling, free writing morning pages, something like that. And then what I normally do is I’ll then for the next hour, is my fiction writing. So I write fiction. So I spend the first hour writing fiction because I find that that’s, that’s the hardest thing for me to do later on. And then the next thing I do is the writing for the work that Sean and I do hear, you know, the emails, the stuff courses, the all of that stuff. And then that takes me up till the next part of the of the day. By the time it gets to three or four o’clock, then I’m done with any version of writing con writing anymore. Um, Sean loves writing at Augustana. Not apparently.

Shawn — 1:43:21
He doesn’t. That was necessity. So yeah, my writing. I wake up every morning, usually about 5:36am. Make a cup of coffee. I usually sit down with one or both of my dogs on the couch. And I read morning pages that’s like that’s, that’s my start to the day I very small handwriting. So three pages long and for morning pages takes me just about an hour. If I’m, if I’m writing, well, I don’t I just write start to finish. And I have a list of question prompts that I use. If I get stuck for somebody, it’s very weird when I get stuck. I’ll scan down and they’ll go past the question and I get I can feel it, like like, and they go back. And then what I’ll often do is I’ll write that question in morning pages, and then I’ll just start answering it. So it’s pretty much a conversation with myself. I think our writing process professionally. It varies. So I’m six out I’m already six hours ahead of me for time. So it creates an environment where sometimes I’m editing because he was writing, sometimes I’m writing and then tomorrow he’ll Edit and then it’ll come back to me. So I don’t know if that was the answer to the question. I hope that’s a helpful answer. For that one, yeah, there we go on to sort of dismiss that. Alright. Let’s see. Question. This is Francis, has a great question here. I read this earlier. For the book funnel you were talking about do book funnels kind of work because they cover for an end cost. So you can keep just keep getting more customers and tiny percent will buy high ticket. That’s the first question and then how come y’all don’t charge more for your stuff? In the Click Funnels world. Everyone is at 997 Plus, but our stuff is way more valuable. Thanks, Francis. I’ll tackle the book funnel part as I just did a million of them. And then Andre, I’ll hand off the pricing question to you because we’ve talked about that a lot. Okay, book funnel. If you look at a really good book funnel, Mike Gillette is a guy to go look at his book funnel, he’s I don’t know his entire backstory. But it’s gi le TT if you go to Mike Gillette’s book funnel. And if you if you look at the book funnel, and then think to yourself, oh my god, this guy is doing it’s so much work to sell it a 995 book. That’s when you realize that the book funnel is not selling the 99, the 995 book, the book final is selling the second upsell, that’s that’s that’s the first change of mind. I just saw your chat, Francis put an E at the end of July. So when you’re creating a book funnel, a really good book funnel sells the upsells. That’s how it read that that’s really Todd brown talks about this a lot in Todd is.

Todd has the Todd has described this in a way that’s real, really genius. And the first time I heard it, I disagreed with him. And then as I thought about it, it’s really brilliant in the central idea is this, when you have a book for any front end funnel, there are really two numbers that you care about cost per acquisition, how much does it cost you to get a customer and average order value? How much did they buy, on average, when they become a customer, Todd’s insight is most things that reduce CPA are difficult, not reliable, and really hard to move the needle on that like all the conversion rate optimisation stuff is really hard. However, increasing average order value is infinitely easier than decreasing cost per acquisition. So what that leads to when people internalize that idea is that you create a quote unquote, book funnel. But there’s so much put into that book funnel that you’re not actually selling the book, you’re selling the second upsell the $250 upsell to drive up your average order value, so that you’re at least at breakeven, which is getting customers for free, who you are then trying to sell a core offer to where you might even be making money on the front end. So that’s the short answer to the book funnel question. Second question over to Andre, how can we don’t charge more for our stuff? Most people 9997 plus $2,000 is the is the sort of current conventional price price range, primarily because half of that needs to go to the affiliate. So a couple of between 1002 $1,000 and we sell for half that. So why do we do that Andre?

André — 1:49:54
It may come as a surprise that we don’t actually look at what other marketers do. We do work with feels right for us. And one of the things that, you know, everybody knows that we optimize for, which is happy customers and in our minds, charging $500, for all of our courses, just seems like it’s ridiculous value we want, we want customers to be surprised and delighted when they make a purchase at everything they’re getting. So that’s the balance, it’s, you know, we don’t want to sell our stuff for $100, because that’ll be done. But 500 seems like it’s just ridiculous. Barely. And as part of our, our worldview of, of optimizing for happy customers, many, many, many of our customers a large percentage of them, a decent percentage of them, purchase all of our courses. Because, you know, one of the reasons is because it’s such ridiculous value $4,000. Well, there weren’t all of them at that point. That would be different if each course cost $2,000. I mean, we could sell them for $2,000. But, you know, again, in our world, there will be done, because we don’t think that is the is the best path for us to generate as many happy customers as possible. So 500 is just the number that showed up. That just felt right. And we’re stuck with it. So every single course that gets created, when it’s finally released, it’ll be $500. Well, 495, that’s, that’s the price. If it’s unreleased, and it’s better, then we, we’ve even things that even more stupid, and we sell them for 200 and something or 300. And something. And again, that’s that’s just because that’s how we want to show up and, and do our stuff. works for us.

Shawn — 1:51:59
Yeah, I don’t think I really have much to add to that. I mean, it’s just it’s a, it comes down to this, I think the thing that people forget all the time I teach to entrepreneurship class, entrepreneurship classes at Vermont tech. And one of the things I mentioned to students a lot is, when you become an entrepreneur, you not only are not only playing a games, if you’re imagining chess, you’re not you’re not playing a game based on somebody else’s rules, what you realize as an entrepreneur is you get to make the rules. And there’s there’s no rule that says, We need to charge. And there’s there’s just there’s there’s no rule, we get to decide, like that’s part of the freedom of being entrepreneurs. And you know that the price point where Andre landed before I showed up, and what we’ve talked about this makes perfect sense to me is that the person who pays 495 ish running around there, that person is making a significant commitment. And we recognize that would we would it change the nature of things, if we doubled that commitment, probably not for that person, we would, what would probably happen on balance is that fewer people would get access to what we’re creating. And that doesn’t feel right. At the same time. $200 wouldn’t wouldn’t have the same poll for that person, they wouldn’t feel like they’ve they’ve invested enough that it’s worth drawing them into it. For some reason that 495 price point in that area, it feels like that it like that it’s in the person who is buying best interest. And that’s really what we’re optimizing for, yes, we want to make enough money that we can put a roof over our head and pay for some of our bad habits and things like that. But that’s not what motivates us. And if if we’re really going to be true to what we teach, which is critically important for both of us that we are, that what you see is what you get, you’re never going to find out when you pull the curtain back that we’ve been making up some things along the way, there’s to ensure there’s never a chance of that happening, then everything has to have that same level of integrity in the pricing. It seems to be in that neighborhood where it it can be we realize it can be a stretch. But when somebody makes that decision and makes that stretch, we want them to feel like almost like stunned, like, like they really got so much value from it and that that’s something money can’t buy from our perspective. So that’s, uh, that’s all, I think. All right, let’s go to I keep thinking we’re getting close but or not. So I’ll answer this question that Cohen asked. I really hope I’m pronouncing your name correctly. To Shawn, can you add Can you tell more about getting that silver thread out of the work we did making a Facebook ad to offer emails and how to tie that to our ideal customer. problems and needs. I think I mentioned that in the recording for in, in week five for the promotional emails. That is just for background purposes, I have stolen that idea from animal trackers, they’re like some of the particularly native Aboriginal, very sophisticated animal trackers often talk about, when they’re, they’re following an animal, they’re, they’re seeing a silver line like that. Like they’re, I don’t know, I don’t know if they mean they’re imagining it, but that between them in the animal that they’re tracking there is there’s a silver line that connects them. And I don’t know, I don’t know anything about the nuances of that at all. But for some reason that that idea has resonated with me. And when I think about writing, or crafting a message, like from Facebook, ad to landing page through seven different emails, I’m imagining that there is this line that connects everything from beginning to end. So when we, I believe is week zero, or we, I guess, week one with the thematic arc, you can think of it as that line.

It so that there’s, but it’s not that it’s like if for me, if I were to, if I were to take like if somebody handed me your writing, and said, here’s all my stuff. And this is really what we talked about, but a lot in the in the bonus chapter that we released this morning. But if someone were to hand me all of their stuff, I would want to know, structurally, I would want to be able to draw that line from like.to.to dot through their work to say like, oh, here’s the thread that connects everything I know, and leading them to this destination on the treasure map, and there’s an X on it. And then I know their way over here on the beach, they just landed on the island. And what I’m my writing is doing is I’m taking them across that trail, those are my ideas or that trail that leads them to the X on the on the treasure map. And it’s I wish I could explain it. But I can see it in my mind. I can sort of sense it. But it’s that how does each thing that you’re saying there’s a frame that you’ve set. And then there’s this world that you’ve created. That’s kind of the treasure map, that’s the island. And then you’re each idea that you’re expressing should lead from one to the next to the next to the next so that when they when they arrive at the X on the map their beer offer. It just feels like that. Of course, that’s where they should they arrive, like everything you’ve said has been leading there all along. And the moment it’s revealed, it’s almost like this exhale, like, of course, this is what it’s been all about. Like that’s, that’s the feeling I’m going for. Okay, now now to pronounce your name. So sorry. I don’t know, if you want to share any thoughts on that? That was my metaphor. I don’t know, Andre, you may think about that entirely differently. No metaphor that you use.

André — 1:58:04
I love that. That metaphor. Yeah, the only thing I’ll add is that, you know, we spend, Shawn and I spend a lot of time thinking out loud to each other. And brainstorming, coming up with with themes for the various things we were going to create. And it’s always more rich and more valuable when we’re doing it together. As opposed to when I used to do this myself. Or if there’s not a collaboration partner to bounce ideas from, and it’s just in my head, those ideas are never as good. I mean, you can, you can still come up with with the same or similar themes. Sure. It’s just the process is very different. And it feels more emergent, when it’s when it’s two people or three people, I suppose. But I suppose at some point, then it gets too many. So I think if anything, find a writing partner, or collaboration partner or something, or just customers or a group of people that you hang out with, and then just share your idea and have a brainstorm and, and that backwards and forwards is going to create emergent ideas that then will inform themes and other you know, angles that you can use for your for your, you know, for the work you’re doing

Shawn — 1:59:31
today, and I think your conversation we had yesterday is a great example of this. We don’t want to give away too much top secret. It’s a open loop, open loop open loop, but it’s what we were working on the structure of something very important. That’s coming out soon it’s going to be amazing. But it was two hours. I mean, really two hours of what if we had stopped anywhere between one hour and an hour and 45 minutes. And anybody saw what we accomplished, they would think that we were just monkeys hammering away on a keyboard it was, we were it was just we were floundering. But what what was happening is it was it was leading to a place where all of the pieces like a Rubik’s Cube, were turning and turning and turning. And all of a sudden, the last one clicked and was like, there it is. And from that emerged, the structure like that made sense and was congruent and could frame everything we’re doing on an enormous project. It just, it’s so it’s that it’s something about that, like, don’t give up on it early. If you’re frustrated, when you’re, you’re trying to find it. That’s, that’s, that’s the name of the game. It happens. But it’s some point, it kind of clicks. And you could actually do this and you could, I know like John McPhee, the nonfiction writer, he talks about in his book, draft number four, but when he, he writes, or he types his ideas, which are probably what we would call story beats, but he types those, and then he cuts the paper up into little slips of paper. So it’s like one idea per slip of paper, and then it gets ignored. Like he gets a four by eight sheet of plywood in his office. And the book, he arranges the book, with those little slips of paper and like makes a book outline on it’s not the writing, it’s the ideas, like, so if you have I mean, it sounds crazy to print out all of your work in a digital world and set it up. But it would be interesting. If you really want to pressure test your work, print out everything that you’ve written, cut them into ideas, and then proceed to the ideas flow. Do they make sense? You do you say the same thing. 12 different times.
