Okay, next question. This is from Robin, at what, at? What kind of product price point does your worldbuilding framework start? It seems more geared toward high priced products. Andre, your thoughts on that?

André — 2:02:28
Maybe I’m not understanding the question. But our world building isn’t tied to, to a to a price point. It’s, it’s, it’s the it’s the narratives and the, and the stuff we put out there. So for us, our world building can start on external web websites in on podcast interviews, but really, it starts on our website. So we’ve got hundreds and hundreds of 1000s of words of, of front end website content where somebody can read without opting in without having any transaction with us, other than them interacting with our content. So that’s our, that’s our, our framing or our worldview for what we’re building is and and where it starts, and then it leads to an email opt in, or a purchase. And then then the will building continues in email in the courses that that we create. It’s, that’s all part of the world building. You know. So I think that’s, in fact, the, the video that’s inside of the bonus lesson four that we share today. The one with RR Martin, I think that’s his name. But you know, that whole video is most of the video is on, on how he’s talking about what we’re building. And that’s really I’ve just, we’ve just taken that metaphor and applied it to, to the stuff that we create, which is our version of world building. That yes, there’s products that are part of that, that that world, but it doesn’t have to start like that. I think that’s, that’s, that’s my answer. That’s my final answer.

Shawn — 2:04:21
And I like Seth Godin is, I think in an interview at one point said that a brand is a promise in your different different brands make different promises. I think he uses the example of like the Hilton Hotel versus like a cheap hotel, it’s a different promise. And we build worlds. I think we’re, we’re making a promise. And that it’s a it’s a context specific promise, but it’s this idea that when you’re interested in X, Robin is the person who, like that’s the that’s the person that’s that’s what we want somebody to think of. Right? So the world that you’re building and the framing for that world is when somebody He is interested in the topic where you are the expert that they look to you first, and they trust over time, their their barriers to trusting you are dropping significantly because you’ve demonstrated time and time again that you’re the expert and that you have their best interest at heart. So when we’re, when we think about world building, it’s not necessarily a time bound event, as much as it is a way of showing up in the world where you are The logical conclusion to a question that’s asked about your expertise. So if for me, if I decided to get back on my bicycle at some point, and wanted to go fast, I would reach out to Jonathan Baker, because I know in my world, the person who is the brand promise to me of Jonathan Baker is he is the person who knows how to train people to go faster on a bicycle. That’s his promise, the world in which he inhabits is that world. And that’s I think that’s what a lot of world building is about is that we’re, it’s a way to contextualize and make sense of another person and say, when you have this set of challenges, or you want this type of outcome, or you want to learn more about this subject, this is the person who you go to ideally, first, or this is one of several people you go to, I think that that’s how I would describe it. Alright. Next question, Brian. Any advice on how to best target audiences on Facebook using the momentum builder workshops? Should we make our Facebook targeting more broad? Short advice, best advice I can give you on Facebook is make a large audience with a clear optimization event. And let Facebook, our Facebook algorithm work on your behalf. So Facebook’s algorithm 400,000 data points, if you if in the challenge, of course is what do you optimize for, in the short term I would optimize for leads. In the longer term, if you have enough volume, I would optimize for purchases. The challenge with optimizing for purchases is you know, as of Monday, we think the iOS 14 updates going to drop on Monday, which is going to make it that’s going to sort of have wreak havoc on a bunch of Facebook tracking issues. And it’s also Facebook is reducing its conversion window down to seven days. So it’s unlikely in the scenarios that we’re describing, you’re going to get somebody to become a customer in seven days. And that momentum builder workshop structure. So knowing that I would optimize for leads the problem with leads, it’s not that’s a precursor to what you actually want to have happen. So I wouldn’t get too caught up in that but broad audience optimized for leads. But don’t try to optimize for leads in your the way you’re writing. Let Facebook optimize for leads to find your audience within the big audience. But don’t do anything to kind of goose that where you’re you’re offering a lead magnet or some sort of incentive don’t do any of that. And I think that’s the sort of middle ground combination. All right, next question. Product again, this is the question is I’m a copywriter with a full time job with an agency I write quite a lot from my clients, which leaves me a little energy and also little to write copy for myself. I hope you would agree that writing is hard work. Any suggestions on managing this energy? A few suggestions. Andre, any suggestions on energy management?

André — 2:08:36
Yeah, I don’t have a good answer for this. Other than, perhaps, you know, treat yourself as a as a client. So you you become a client for yourself. And if there’s no slots, and if you decided that you can only do 10 clients slots, I’m just using that as a as an idea. And 10 client slots are filled up. Well, you don’t have any more time to do anything else. So when there’s when there’s nine slots, eight slots, well, now you can consider yourself as you can slot yourself in there now and then shedule out the time as if you were doing it for a client. And if we yourself don’t do client work, so it’s difficult for me to to answer that in any other way.

Shawn — 2:09:32
Yeah, so the way I would think about this, and I’ve actually dealt with this in the past, too, so have some thoughts on it. First thing I would do is figure out when you have peak energy in general, just at and that’s unique to you. And broadly speaking you it’s probably in the morning or it’s probably late at night, those seem to be the two the two extremes. For me for example, I’m a far better writer in the morning, although I get a weird say Can’t wind late to occasionally. But the reason you need to do that is once you know when you are at your creative best, then you want to set aside that period of time. Even if it’s only 30 minutes of that time, whatever it is, you want to set aside that time to write every single day and into write consistently. Don’t worry about how much you write each day, just write every day. And it’s consistency over intensity. And what you’ll find is that the compounding interest of time passing produces results far beyond what you would expect when you sit down one day, you just feel like you didn’t accomplish anything, it’s just your day to day don’t feel like you accomplished anything, and you look back. And over the course of a month, you wrote 1000s and 1000s of words. So consistency over intensity, aligned when you write with your peak creative time. All of this, though, is within the context of what are you optimizing for, right. So if you’re optimizing for being a copywriter who produces great work for the agency, then then that’s a very different story, then you need to align your peak creative time with the most demanding clients that you have, or the clients that produce the most reward, however, you’re measuring that reward. But if you’re optimizing for creating something for yourself, that changes things that and you have to respect that. In the last thing I’ve mentioned, here is it everything that you want. So you know, from an energy management perspective, you know, in there, there, certainly, there are ways you can hack it, you know, caffyns, great ally, nicotine is a great ally. And that’s done smoke it, but other ways that consumer you can boost your energy. That’s, I don’t really recommend that. I mean, lion’s mane is good for like clarity of mind. There are lots of ways you can kind of you can hack it, but where the power is where your power exists, is in making a decision. And years ago, I someone explained this, I assume this is true, it may be it’s not true. But the Latin root of the word decision is, means to cut. So a decision is to cut off all alternatives. And the moment you make a decision that you’re going to write every day, and you cut off all alternatives come hell or high water, you write every day. And the moment you do that, everything changes. You don’t have to, you know, it’s all this bullshit about it’s 60 days to create a habit, the hell with habits, I don’t give a shit about habits, I care about decisions. If I decide that I’m going to write every day, I’m going to write every day, I don’t need to wait until my nervous system catches up and says, hey, it’s a habit I can I’ve made and broken habits so many times in my life that it’s, it’s ridiculous. So, but decisions, all of our power exists within a decision. In the moment you make the decision that you’re a writer, and you’re going to write 30 minutes every day, and they’re in your no exceptions. 100% much easier than 99%. No exceptions. That’s where all of your power, then you don’t need to worry about energy. You don’t need to worry do I feel like writing now with that, right? If every day at 9am, or whatever, so you get up early at 6am. If between 6am and 6:30am. Every single day you write, and there’s no issue, there’s never an exception. You’re a writer, at that point, you’re doing the work, period, you need to manage anything, you don’t need to think about anything, but you’re asking to chair, fingers on the keyboard, you’re a writer, period. Alright. And walk in nature when you do it to fall in the chat. Okay. Let’s see. Can we use our funnel to do Google ads instead of Facebook ads and what part of our funnel became use the same? So in the reveal, as you resolve that tension, you also have to create more tension for what you’re actually going to sell them later, which are the results that this framework creates. So as you wouldn’t, so if you can imagine that you’re you’re hinting at your so you’re creating a little tension you’re hinting at, and not completely revealing what the framework is, and then you’re describing but not completely resolving that tension. And at some point, you’re you’re doing the masterful thing that happens in good writing, which is you are resolving the tension by describing this framework you’ve been hinting at all the time, but actually increasing the overall tension. Because that framework is the whole story. It’s part of that the actual story that you’re revealing to them through the purchase of your product. So that’s that’s how, that’s how I would think about how and when to reveal that hint at it, explore it a little show, like just you’re showing you’re taking a peek at it, peek at it, you’ve revealed it, but the big reveal is that it’s not the framework, it’s how their life changes on the other side of of having that framework, which is what you’re going to explain to them next. Now you’ve just jacked up the tension again, and you continue to pull them forward. Assuming any of that, and

because we’re optimizing for creating value for our audience, it’s really short sighted, for us to assume that everybody only wants our finished thinking. It’s idea slams into lots of work. Lots of thinking, lots of strategy, lots of three steps forward, two steps back. tangents like that, so much goes on in between that, I think the the courses we create, certainly we believe they’re very valuable. But there’s also so much value, seeing the reality of what it takes to do that, so that it doesn’t just seem like it’s just hey, they have some ideas. And then they write a course. And it’s sunshine and unicorns, life’s not like that. And we don’t ever want anybody to think it’s that easy. Because we’re not in the lying to you that it’s easy business. We don’t sell easy, we sell reality. And we don’t sell this, Hey, we tried this thing, and it worked once. So therefore anybody can do it.
